item.title: A new sunscreen finally survives the glare of FDA bureaucracy
item.description: A 1938 law has prevented Americans from accessing better sunscreen products.

item.title: An anniversary cage match? America has seen this humiliating spectacle before.
item.description: There are echoes of 1926 in Trump’s attempt to celebrate a national milestone with a prizefight.

item.title: Harris, Newsom, Buttigieg, Shapiro … Early polls overlook a name.
item.description: The progressive lane still is wide open — and Ro Khanna knows it.

item.title: Why Maine Democrats should back Susan Collins
item.description: Compromising on character always comes with a cost.

item.title: These academic disciplines risk forgetting their core task
item.description: Some academics don’t believe in truth. What are they researching for?

item.title: Pay $50,000 to do her job? This nurse practitioner is suing.
item.description: States like Missouri need more health care providers. Loosening licensing rules can help fill the gap.

item.title: What else is caught in the Strait of Hormuz choke point
item.description: The closure is making fertilizer more expensive and hurting food production.

item.title: Why I will use my Fifth Amendment rights before Congress today
item.description: Silence in response to bad-faith action is not retreat — it is a bedrock American right.

item.title: Do aliens exist? I asked an astrophysicist.
item.description:
Are humans alone in the universe? The answer is more complicated than science fiction might suggest. Scientists have discovered billions upon billions of potentially habitable planets. But if life is possible elsewhere, why haven’t we found it?
Host Megan McArdle talks with astrophysicist Adam Frank, author of “The Little Book of Aliens.” They discuss the search for alien life, why intelligent civilizations may be harder to find than microbes and the limits of what UFOs can reveal.

item.title: Are Graham Platner's scandals helping him?
item.description: Are Graham Platner's controversies actually helping him in the Maine Senate race? In this episode of "Make It Make Sense," Kate Andrews talks with Carine Hajjar about Platner's rise, his refusal to drop out amid multiple scandals, and why Democrats are still standing behind him.

item.title: Mamdani’s unconstitutional housing solution digs a deeper hole
item.description: The best way to alleviate the crisis is to build homes, not seize them.

item.title: Europe is becoming Iran’s playground for terrorism
item.description: Tehran is taking a page out of Moscow’s playbook for hybrid aggression in the West.

item.title: How to stop the next pandemic before it begins
item.description: Deadly pathogens can come from nature — or from the lab. That’s why risky research needs guardrails.

item.title: Big Tobacco didn’t just sell cigarettes. It shaped what Americans eat.
item.description: New research shows how cigarette companies brought their addiction playbook to the food industry.

item.title: A ‘forgotten world war’ helped forge America
item.description: The Revolutionary War succeeded because of allies at home and abroad.

item.title: Elon Musk took out his chainsaw. Government took out its checkbook.
item.description: The SpaceX IPO lays bare Musk’s greatest contradiction.
